evidence: lib/presentation/providers/app_provider.dart concentrates multiple subsystem responsibilities in one provider: server profile CRUD/persistence, network client configuration, local runtime lifecycle, diagnostics, health polling, and UI notification state transitions.

lib/presentation/providers/settings_provider.dart combines UI-facing settings state with infrastructure concerns (Dio networking, package info/update checks, file system/open-file install flow, and server config synchronization), creating wide dependency fan-in at the presentation boundary.

Platform-specific file action flows in lib/presentation/services/file_part_action_service_io.dart and lib/presentation/services/file_part_action_service_web.dart replicate top-level decision orchestration (URL scheme routing and fallback behavior), increasing cross-platform drift risk.
ChatProvider is split across many `part` files but remains a single mutable object with broad responsibilities and many injected use cases (`lib/presentation/providers/chat_provider.dart`).

Extension parts directly mutate shared private state (`_messages`, `_selectedModelId`, `_providers`, sync flags), so decomposition is organizational rather than contractual (`lib/presentation/providers/chat_provider/chat_provider_core.dart`, `lib/presentation/providers/chat_provider/chat_provider_message_state_ops.dart`).

ChatPage is similarly decomposed into many extensions over `_ChatPageState`, with each extension reading/writing shared UI/runtime fields (`lib/presentation/pages/chat_page.dart`, `lib/presentation/pages/chat_page/chat_page_file_runtime.dart`, `lib/presentation/pages/chat_page/chat_page_workspace_controller.dart`).

Message part handling requires coordinated edits in both model parsing and UI dispatch switches, which increases indirection cost for each new part type (`lib/data/models/chat_message_model.dart`, `lib/presentation/widgets/chat_message/chat_message_part_dispatch.dart`).

No dedicated tests were found for `lib/core/di/injection_container.dart` (no `injection_container` test targets), despite startup wiring and `_loadLocalConfig()` fallback/JSON parsing behavior being runtime-critical.

No unit tests were found for `lib/presentation/utils/shortcut_binding_codec.dart` covering platform-modifier mapping (`mod` -> Cmd/Ctrl), token normalization, unknown token handling, or key-event serialization.

No direct tests were found for `lib/data/models/chat_realtime_model.dart` parser edge cases (payload-wrapped vs flat events, mixed key styles like `sessionID`/`messageID`, default fallbacks for malformed input).

`lib/domain/entities/server_profile.dart` serialization/deserialization behavior is exercised indirectly via provider/data-source tests, but entity-level contract tests are missing for `fromJson` defaults and `copyWith` invariants.

lib/presentation/pages/chat_page.dart centralizes many unrelated concerns in one state object (UI scaffolding, notification tap handling, background/foreground timers, file explorer caches, timeline compaction, keyboard shortcuts), while behavior is split across many `part` extensions that still mutate the same shared state.

lib/presentation/providers/chat_provider/chat_provider_core.dart::_initializeProvidersInternal combines provider fetch, remote-selection merge, model/provider prioritization heuristics, agent/variant resolution, and persistence writes in one long orchestration path.

lib/presentation/services/local_opencode_server_runtime_io.dart combines process lifecycle control, environment diagnostics, installation strategy orchestration, release download/extract, filesystem probing, and recommendation generation in a single concrete runtime class.

Multiple catch blocks swallow exceptions and return benign defaults without preserving cause/context, e.g. `android_background_alert_worker.dart` (`_fetchSessionStatusById`, `_fetchSessionMetadata`, `_fetchPermissionRequests`, `_fetchQuestionRequests`) and `settings_provider.dart` (`syncNotificationsFromServerConfig`, cleanup catches in `_installAndroid`).

Error signaling style is mixed across adjacent presentation/domain flows: thrown exceptions (`app_provider.dart` `normalizeServerUrl` throws `FormatException`), nullable error-string returns (`settings_provider.dart` `updateShortcut` returns `String?`), and `Either<Failure, T>` (`chat_provider.dart` `_runSessionInsightRequest`).

Failure modeling drops technical context at conversion boundaries: `failures.dart` stores only `message` and optional `code`, while `chat_provider.dart` maps unexpected exceptions into `UnknownFailure('Unexpected error...')` without retaining original exception/stack for downstream handling.

findings.summary: AppProvider is a coupling hotspot across runtime, networking, persistence, and UI state.
evidence: AppProvider imports and coordinates infrastructure services directly (runtime process control, health probes via Dio, local persistence) while also exposing UI state and view-facing actions.
Methods for server profile lifecycle, runtime diagnostics/start-stop, and health polling coexist in one class, so unrelated changes touch the same module.
Error/reporting behavior is also embedded through AppLogger calls, further centralizing cross-cutting concerns in a presentation provider.
findings.suggestion: Split AppProvider into orchestrated collaborators: e.g., ServerProfileManager, LocalServerRuntimeController, and ServerHealthMonitor; keep AppProvider as thin composition/state adapter. Move runtime/network orchestration behind domain-facing service interfaces and inject them.

findings.summary: SettingsProvider crosses presentation, update/install, and remote-config boundaries directly.
evidence: SettingsProvider imports infrastructure APIs (Dio, dart:io, path_provider, open_filex, package_info_plus) and performs update check/download/install logic in the same class that manages UI settings toggles.
The provider also handles server-backed notification syncing and persistence concerns directly, combining distinct module boundaries in one type.
ExperienceSettings is a broad entity and the provider exposes/updates many orthogonal concerns through one mutable surface, increasing transitive coupling.
findings.suggestion: Extract UpdateManager and NotificationConfigSync services/use-cases from SettingsProvider, leaving the provider focused on presentation state mapping and user intents. Keep platform/file-install logic in dedicated infrastructure services with narrow interfaces.

findings.summary: IO and Web file action services duplicate orchestration logic, inviting boundary drift.
evidence: Both services implement similar top-level `handleFilePartAction` flow (parse URL, branch by scheme, fallback handling), but each keeps its own control path and messages.
Shared helpers exist, yet decision sequencing remains duplicated across platform modules, so behavior can diverge over time.
Cross-platform parity for attachment handling is enforced by convention instead of a single orchestration contract.
findings.suggestion: Move scheme-routing orchestration into one shared policy layer that returns platform intents (open-link/save-inline/open-local/unsupported), and keep io/web files as thin executors of platform-specific side effects only.

findings.summary: ChatProvider part split adds indirection but not ownership boundaries
evidence: `ChatProvider` aggregates many concerns (session lifecycle, provider/model selection, realtime sync, queueing, auto-title, cache) behind one class-level state bag.
Part extensions mutate the same private fields directly, so modules are tightly coupled through hidden shared state rather than explicit contracts.
Changing one concern can require understanding unrelated private invariants across multiple extension files.
findings.suggestion: Extract concern-focused collaborators (for example: `SessionStore`, `SelectionState`, `RealtimeCoordinator`, `MessageStateReducer`) as separate classes with explicit interfaces; keep `ChatProvider` as orchestration/composition only.

findings.summary: ChatPage extensions create a distributed method mesh over one giant state object
evidence: `_ChatPageState` owns a large set of mutable runtime/UI fields while behavior is spread across many extension part files.
Extensions read/write shared flags and timers (`_projectScopeTransitionTask`, `_backgroundRealtimeHoldTimer`, file-context maps), so file-level separation does not reduce coupling.
This structure raises navigation and change cost because behavior for one user flow is fragmented across multiple extension files.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit controller/state objects per concern (workspace context, file explorer runtime, foreground policy, composer status) and pass narrow dependencies into each, replacing extension-based access to the full `_ChatPageState`.

findings.summary: Message part abstraction requires multi-file switch edits for new part types
evidence: `MessagePartModel.toDomain()` uses a large `switch (PartType)` with type-specific parsing and casting.
UI rendering repeats part-type branching in `_buildMessagePart()` with manual casts and special-case visibility rules.
Adding/changing a part type requires synchronized edits across parsing and rendering switches, increasing breakage risk.
findings.suggestion: Replace hard-coded switches with a `PartType` registry (parser + renderer adapter per part type) so extension points are localized and new part types do not require coordinated edits across unrelated files.

findings.summary: ChatInputWidget exposes a wide parameter bag that couples caller and internals
evidence: `ChatInputWidget` constructor carries many booleans/callbacks and behavior toggles (query handlers, stop handling, attachments, context item hooks, controller).
Call site in timeline builder must wire numerous lambdas and flags, which indicates low-cohesion API and high integration friction.
Feature growth is likely to keep expanding the constructor surface instead of using stable abstractions.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a cohesive input contract (for example `ChatComposerConfig` + `ChatComposerActions`) and collapse behavior toggles into typed policies, reducing call-site wiring and improving reuse/testing.

findings.summary: Startup DI/config loading path has no focused tests for parsing/fallback behavior
evidence: `init()` and `_loadLocalConfig()` perform runtime-critical profile decoding and base URL/auth selection, but there is no dedicated test target for injection container behavior.
Failure paths in profile JSON parsing are handled silently with fallback logic; regressions here can misconfigure networking at app startup.
Existing provider/data-source tests do not validate DI startup ordering and local-config bootstrap integration end-to-end.
findings.suggestion: Add focused tests for `injection_container.dart` that verify: valid profile selection by `activeServerId`, malformed JSON fallback, legacy config fallback, and auth propagation into `DioClient` during initialization.

findings.summary: Shortcut codec parsing/serialization contract is untested and platform-sensitive
evidence: The codec contains behavior-dependent branches (`mod` mapping by platform, modifier ordering, token normalization) without dedicated tests.
`fromKeyEvent` relies on `HardwareKeyboard` modifier state and key-label translation, which is fragile without deterministic tests.
Shortcut parsing result (`SingleActivator?`) directly affects command binding in chat/settings flows.
findings.suggestion: Create unit tests for codec round-trips (`normalize` + `parse` + `formatForDisplay`) and key-event serialization, including macOS/non-macOS `mod` behavior, unknown token rejection, and comma/special-key handling.

findings.summary: Realtime event model parsing lacks tests for API shape variants and fallback defaults
evidence: Model parsing supports multiple payload shapes (`payload` wrapper vs top-level fields) and mixed key conventions (`sessionID`, `messageID`, `callID`) but no direct model tests were found.
Several fields default silently to empty strings/idle states, which can mask upstream API drift if unvalidated.
Realtime behavior is high-impact and cross-module; parser regressions can degrade event handling without obvious runtime crashes.
findings.suggestion: Add table-driven tests for `ChatEventModel`, `SessionStatusModel`, `ChatPermissionRequestModel`, and `ChatQuestionRequestModel` covering valid payload variants, malformed payloads, mixed key casing, and explicit assertions on fallback/default behavior.

findings.summary: ChatPage state owns too many responsibilities behind part-file splits
evidence: The root state in chat_page.dart defines large cross-domain mutable state (scrolling, notifications, file context, composer status, compaction caches, keyboard flow) and many timers/controllers.
Runtime extensions in separate files still depend on and mutate the same monolithic private state, so decomposition is mostly physical-file split rather than ownership split.
findings.suggestion: Introduce dedicated controllers with explicit boundaries (for example TimelineController, FileExplorerController, ComposerStatusController, NotificationRoutingController) and have ChatPage compose them via a thin coordinator; move state and side effects out of `_ChatPageState` so each controller can be tested and evolved independently.

findings.summary: Provider init path mixes data loading, policy selection, and persistence side effects
evidence: _initializeProvidersInternal in chat_provider_core.dart performs multiple phases in one method: fetch providers, reconcile remote selection, choose provider/model by several fallback heuristics, resolve agent/variant, then persist selection state.
Adjacent provider extensions already handle realtime/event-reducer responsibilities, indicating ChatProvider has broad orchestration scope and weakly separated policy boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Split initialization into explicit pipeline stages with small units (`loadProviderSnapshot`, `resolveSelectionPolicy`, `applyRemoteOverrides`, `persistSelectionState`) and extract selection policy into a pure resolver object that returns a decision payload; keep side effects in a final apply step.

findings.summary: Local opencode runtime class bundles process control, install, and diagnostics
evidence: _IoLocalOpencodeServerRuntime contains startup/shutdown, environment probing, dependency checks, install method strategy, GitHub release discovery, archive download/extract, and recommendation text assembly.
ServersSettingsSection consumes this operational surface as one feature, but the implementation boundary couples operational policies with low-level process and file/network primitives.
findings.suggestion: Decompose into focused collaborators: `LocalServerProcessManager`, `LocalServerEnvironmentProbe`, `LocalServerInstaller`, and `LocalServerRecommendationPolicy`; keep `LocalOpencodeServerRuntime` as a facade that coordinates these components.

findings.summary: Several runtime/network paths swallow exceptions and return null/empty results, making failures indistinguishable from legitimate empty states.
evidence: `_fetchSessionStatusById` returns `null` on non-404 Dio errors and generic catch; callers cannot distinguish transport failure from unavailable status.
`_fetchSessionMetadata`, `_fetchPermissionRequests`, and `_fetchQuestionRequests` return empty structures on catch, collapsing failure into normal-empty semantics.
`syncNotificationsFromServerConfig` catches and ignores all errors; settings silently remain stale without surfaced state.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a single boundary pattern for these paths: return typed failure objects (or a shared Result) with cause metadata, and reserve empty collections for confirmed empty responses only.

findings.summary: The same user-facing flow family mixes throws, nullable return-errors, and Either-based failures, increasing handling divergence and missed edge cases.
evidence: `app_provider.dart` `normalizeServerUrl` throws `FormatException` for invalid input.
`settings_provider.dart` `updateShortcut` uses `Future<String?>` where `null` means success and string means failure.
`chat_provider.dart` and `chat_repository.dart` predominantly use `Either<Failure, T>` for operational failures.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one error transport per layer boundary (preferably `Either<Failure, T>` for async/domain-facing operations) and adapt throw/nullable APIs at edge adapters only.

findings.summary: Unexpected exceptions are converted into generic failures/messages, reducing diagnosability and consistency of downstream behavior.
evidence: `Failure` types in `failures.dart` do not carry original cause/stack fields.
`_runSessionInsightRequest` catches any exception and returns `UnknownFailure('Unexpected error...')` with no structured propagation of cause.
`chat_page.dart` initialization catch logs and clears provider error, effectively suppressing actionable context in UI state.
findings.suggestion: Extend `Failure` with optional cause and stack metadata (or structured diagnostics id), and preserve these when wrapping unexpected exceptions.

findings.summary: IO and web attachment handlers diverge in error observability: IO logs rich context while web suppresses with `catch (_)`, yielding inconsistent operational visibility.
evidence: IO path logs unexpected decode/save failures with `AppLogger.warn/info` and stack traces.
Web `_downloadInlineAttachment` and MIME inference catches use `catch (_)`, returning generic messages without logging.
Cross-platform behavior yields different diagnostics for equivalent attachment failures.
findings.suggestion: Align both variants to a shared error policy helper: same user-facing messages plus consistent structured logging (including platform + operation stage).

findings.summary: Presentation provider depends on data DTOs, bypassing clean layer boundaries
evidence: Presentation imports `chat_*_model.dart` directly.
Provider code serializes/deserializes via `ChatSessionModel`/`ChatMessageModel` and event DTO models.
findings.suggestion: Move JSON/DTO translation behind a data-facing mapper or cache/event adapter service; keep ChatProvider consuming domain entities and typed domain events only.

findings.summary: Error handling uses conflicting translation strategies across repositories
evidence: Chat datasource throws generic `ServerException` in many paths after `DioException`.
Chat repository maps custom exceptions, while app/project repositories map raw `DioException` via `DioExceptionHandler`.
findings.suggestion: Adopt one shared error-mapping contract (preferably at datasource boundary) that preserves status/context and is reused by all repositories.

findings.summary: Many pass-through use cases add indirection without adding policy
evidence: Use cases frequently forward repository calls with parameter mapping only.
ChatProvider still coordinates extensive behavior directly despite broad use case surface.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate related use cases into cohesive application services (session/message/realtime facets) and reduce ChatProvider constructor surface to policy-bearing interfaces.

findings.summary: Direct clock and delay usage weakens deterministic testing of async flows
evidence: Runtime decisions rely on direct `DateTime.now()` reads.
Retry/reconnect logic uses inline `Future.delayed` and `Timer(...)` scheduling.
findings.suggestion: Introduce injectable `Clock` and scheduler/delay abstractions for provider and datasource async policies, then drive timing behavior via controlled fakes in tests.

findings.summary: Provider extensions blend transport parsing, persistence, and UI state logic
evidence: Event reducer parses low-level map payloads while mutating domain/session state and triggering feedback flows.
Cache extension encodes/decodes JSON DTOs and performs restoration decisions within presentation layer.
findings.suggestion: Extract event translation and cache codec concerns into dedicated collaborators; keep provider focused on state orchestration and user-intent commands.
